coredata.dc:title: Investigation of photoluminescence behavior of reduced graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Wenhui
Wang, Yingmin
Wang, Lifeng
Li, Yan
Li, Yong
Xue, Wendong
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the photoluminescence (PL) behavior of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) was investigated. The rGQDs with different reduced level were obtained by after-reduced electrochemical prepared GQDs using sodium borohydride solution (NaBH4) as reductant. The reduced degree, surface oxygen functional groups, surface defect changes and band gap were detailed investigated through Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution TEM (HRTEM), Atomic force microscopy (AFM), Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Photoluminescence excitation (PLE), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectrum, zeta potential analysis and X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS). The rGQDs showed higher PL emission and improved quantum yields than GQDs. But as the degree of reduction increased, PL emission decreased. Based on these result, we proposed that the PL change result from the surface hybridization state of GQDs. Our work could help to further understanding of PL behavior and mechanism of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Oxygen functional groups

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (a) and size distributions (inserts at top right corner) and HRTEM (inserts at bottom right corner) of GQDs, rGQDs-1, rGQDs-2, and rGQDs-3. AFM image (b) and its height distribution (c) of GQDs, rGQDs-1, rGQDs-2 and rGQDs-3.
The UV–Vis (a), PLE with a emission wavelength of 500 nm (b) and FT-IR (c) spectra of GQDs, rGQDs-1, rGQDs-2, rGQDs-3.
XPS spectrum of GQDs, rGQDs-1, rGQDs-2 and rGQDs-3 (a); O1s content of GQDs, rGQDs-1, rGQDs-2 and rGQDs-3 (b).
Raman spectra (a), ID/IG ratio (b) and zeta potential (c) of GQDs, rGQDs-1, rGQDs-2 and rGQDs-3.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry of GQDs and rGQDs samples modified on GCE electrodes in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 0.5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] and 0.5 mM K4Fe(CN)6•3H2O (b) The cathodic peak current (Ipc) of the samples.
The PL spectra of GQDs and rGQDs with the excitation at 320 nm (a), 340 nm (b), 360 nm (c) and 380 nm (d).
The O1s XPS spectra of GQDs (a), rGQDs-1 (b), rGQDs-2 (c) and rGQDs-3 (d).

head.ce:title: Investigation of photoluminescence behavior of reduced graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No. 30, Xueyuan Rd, Haidian District, Beijing 100083, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the photoluminescence (PL) behavior of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) was investigated. The rGQDs with different reduced level were obtained by after-reduced electrochemical prepared GQDs using sodium borohydride solution (NaBH4) as reductant. The reduced degree, surface oxygen functional groups, surface defect changes and band gap were detailed investigated through Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution TEM (HRTEM), Atomic force microscopy (AFM), Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Photoluminescence excitation (PLE), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectrum, zeta potential analysis and X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS). The rGQDs showed higher PL emission and improved quantum yields than GQDs. But as the degree of reduction increased, PL emission decreased. Based on these result, we proposed that the PL change result from the surface hybridization state of GQDs. Our work could help to further understanding of PL behavior and mechanism of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, graphene [1–3] and its allotropes such as GQDs [4–6], carbon dots(CDs) [7–9], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [10,11] have attracted intensive attention owing to their main graphene structure, large specific surface area, high thermal conductivity and intrinsic electron mobility, excellent biocompatibility and near-infrared (NIR) absorption property. These characters along with their unique PL property of GQDs endow them great potential in the fields of light emitting diodes [12,13], cell imaging [14] and ion detection [15] etc.

GQDs have a single or few layered graphene-like structure, which is mainly composed of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in inner and sp3 hybridized carbon‑oxygen bonds outside, including hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups. The intrinsic fluorescence emission of GQDs is resulted from the π-π* transition of the internal hybrid sp2 region, which controls the width of band gap of the GQDs [16]. Meanwhile, the surface oxygen groups also participate in the formation of the energy level structure, leading to multiple surface state energy levels and corresponding to n-π* transitions under proper irradiation [17]. The fluorescence emission caused by n-π* transitions was usually referred to extrinsic emission [18]. Therefore, now the mechanism of fluorescence of GQDs could be divided into two major categories: one is quantum confinement effect of conjugated sp2 structure, and the other is surface/edge state luminescence [19]. The quantum confinement effect which produces the transition of band gap occurs when the size of material reduced to nanometer especially smaller than or equal to the exciton Bohr radius. Kim et al. fabricated various-size GQDs by hydrothermal method to cutting graphene sheets [20]. It demonstrated that the peak position and the shape of the luminescent spectrum were related to the size of GQDs. For the surface/edge state luminescence, it was mainly affected by the graphene core and its surface oxygen functional groups [21]. These surface states form a series of different energy levels, resulting in a series of emission defect states. Therefore, the GQDs often emit fluorescence that changes with the excitation wavelength. Lingam et al. researched the fluorescence property of GQDs with its edge-states by annealing to passivate the free edges. It suggested that the surface state played a key role on PL mechanism [22].

Considering that GQDs have a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface and its fluorescence is influenced by surface/edge state [23], we adjust the number of surface functional groups of GQDs by adding different concentrations of sodium borohydride solutions. Their PL properties were studied to further provide valuable reference for luminescence mechanism investigation of GQDs.

The GQDs were prepared by cyclic voltammetry method on electrochemical working station (CHI 660D). The working electrode and the counter electrode both were graphite rods (purity 99.99%) and a platinum electrode, respectively. The neutral phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution (0.1 M 40 mL) was used as electrolyte. The scanning voltage was set to 5 V with a scan rate of 0.1 V/s, and the scanning time was 48 h. After scanning, the solution was filtered by a 220 nm filter and subsequently dialyzes using a 3500 Da dialysis bag for three days. The concentration was 0.12 mg/mL.

rGQDs were prepared as follows:NaBH4 solution (0.04 mg/mL) in ethanol was prepared at first. The same GQDs solutions (15 mL 0.12 mg/mL) at glass bottle were added different volumes (0 μL, 20 μL, 30 μL and 40 μL) of NaBH4 solutions. The mixtures were stirring by a magnetic stirring apparatus at room temperature for 24 h. Afterwards, the solutions were dialyzed using a 3500 Da dialysis bag for four days to remove the extra reductant. rGQDs with different reduced degree were named rGQDs-1, rGQDs-2 and rGQDs-3.

The TEM and high resolution TEM was recorded by a JEM-2010 electron microscope. The AFM images were obtained using a Bruker Dimension Icon microscope. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs were recorded by the TU-1900 spectrophotometer. In addition, the PL and PLE spectra were measured by 4500 Fluorimeter. The FT-IR spectra were conducted by a NEXUS spectrometer 670. The surface composition of the samples were obtained by X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) with an Al monochromatic Ka radiation (hυ = 1486.6 eV) source. Raman spectrum was collected using a RM 2000 Microscopic Confocal Raman Spectrometer (laser wavelength: 632.8 nm). The zeta potential was measured by the Zetasizer (Nano-ZS) from Malvern Instruments. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was measured by a relative method using quinine sulfate as the reference. The PLQY (φ) was calculated as follow [24]:
φ =
φ R
I
I R
A R
A
η 2
η R
2
where φ was the PLQY of the testing samples, I was the integrated emission intensity, A is the optical density of samples. η was the refractive index (1.33 for water and 1.42 for H2SO4). The superscript “R” refers to the referenced standard of known PLQYs (quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4, φR = 0.55) [24].

The electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 660 D electrochemical working station (Chenhua Instrument, Shanghai, China).The Cyclic voltammetry curve were recorded with a conventional three-electrode system, by using glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, Pt plate as the counter electrode and the Ag/AgCl as reference electrode. Before each experiment, the GCE electrode, was polished with alumina powders, followed by ultra sonication in ethanol and deionized water for 20 min, thoroughly. A 50 μL of GQDs samples was spread on the pretreated bare GCE using a micropipette and dry naturally in the air. The electrolyte was KCl (0.1 M) containing potassium ferricyanide (0.5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]) and potassium ferrocyanide (0.5 mM K4Fe(CN)6·3H2O). The data was recorded after about four cycles under steady-state conditions.

The TEM images of the GQDs and rGQDs are shown in Fig. 1 . It can be seen that the both GQDs and rGQDs are monodisperse with the average sizes of 4.37 nm, 4.17 nm, 3.13 nm and 3.03 nm, respectively. Along with the reduced level increased from rGQD-1 to rGQD-3, the size of rGQDs decreased. It could be resulted from the crack of graphene sheets inside the GQDs and the remove of edged oxygen groups in the reduced process. For the inside ones, the reduction would result in the breakage of CO bonds. When a large number of CO bonds broken, graphene sheets inside the GQDs would be cracked, and correspondingly the size of rGQDs would be decreased. On the other hand, GQDs contained the graphene sheets and the surface oxygen functional groups. Therefore, the sizes of GQDs include both of them. After reduction, the decrease of surface oxygen functional groups would correspondingly decrease the total size of GQDs. Moreover, from the inset of Fig. 1a, the lattice parameter of GQDs and rGQDs was 0.24 nm, coinciding with the (1120) crystal phase of graphite [25]. It indicated that after reduction rGQDs kept the graphene structure. Fig. 1b and Fig. 1c showed AFM micrographs and height profiles of GQDs and rGQDs. No obvious difference was found in thickness between GQDs and rGQDs (both were within 1–3 nm), implying that the reduction of surface oxygen functional groups do little influence on their thickness.

Although reduction of GQDs decreased their size, no obvious band gap changes can be seen from the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2a) and PLE spectra (Fig. 2b). These results demonstrated that NaBH4 mainly removed edged oxygen groups of GQDs and did little influence on their inside graphene sheet's size, because the band gap mainly depended on their size of intrinsic graphene [25]. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 2c reflected the type of surface oxygen functional group of GQDs and rGQDs, there had epoxy (COC, 1056 cm−1) stretching vibration band, carbonyl (CO, 1736 cm−1) stretching vibration band, and hydroxyl groups (C-OH, 3421 cm−1) stretching vibration band [26,27]. The stretching vibration intensity for these bands of GQDs was stronger than rGQDs, confirming GQDs were indeed reduced.

To further investigate the changes of surface oxygen groups, XPS tests were performed on the GQDs and rGQDs (Fig. 3 ). It can be seen that there are two distinct characteristic peaks of the four samples in XPS spectra at the wavelengths of 284.5 nm and 532.0 nm which are the C1s and O1s peaks, respectively. The peak of O1s gradually weakens and the peak of C1s gradually strengthens with the increase of reduced degree from rGQDs-1 to rGQDs-3. It was clearly demonstrated that surface oxygen functional groups were gradually decreased by reduction.

The Raman spectrum of GQDs and rGQDs were also investigated at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 4a. There are two dominant peaks at the range of 800–2000 cm−1: D band (around 1314 cm−1) and G band (around 1595 cm−1). The D peak is ascribed to vibrations of carbon atoms and some dangling bonds in vacancy, functional groups or other topological defects; the G band is related to the E2g vibrations mode of sp2-bonded carbon atoms [28,29]. ID/IG of GQDs can reflect the structural changes of the GQDs backbone, Fig. 4b is a line graph of ID/IG intensity ratio. The ID/IG intensity ratio of GQDs, rGQDs-1, rGQDs-2, and rGQDs-3 are 1.07, 0.96, 0.94, and 0.9, respectively. The gradually reduce in ID/IG value implied that the degree of defects of GQDs decreased. It is caused by the GQDs were reduced to a different degree. Furthermore, it is known that GQDs showed negative because of the presence of surface oxygen functional groups. So, zeta potential measurement was induced to indicate the reduced degree of GQDs. In Fig. 4c, the potential values of four GQDs samples are negative. However, along with the reduced degree increased, the potential values were decreased from −30.65 mV, to −12.82 mV, further suggesting that disintegrate of surface oxygen functional groups of GQDs after reduction.

Oxygen functional groups have significant effect on graphene and its allotropes materials, hence, it is very important to research the electrochemical properties of GQDs and rGQDs. Here, cyclic voltammetry measurements were introduced to study the impact of oxygenated functional groups in GQDs. In Fig. 5 , it is obvious that the bare GCE electrode show a higher current value than other samples for the redox reaction. In other words, Ipc values of all samples were lower than the bare GCE sample. This phenomenon was mainly attributed to the semiconductor characteristic of GQDs. With a large amount of oxygen functional groups, they could as an insulating layer on the GCE electrode surface. Due to their high surface charge, the ferricyanide ions are repelled at the electrode surface, thereby limited electrochemical reactions [30]. Besides, Ipc was raised with the degree of reduction increased. That is to say, contributing to rGQDs containing less oxygen functional groups, more ferricyanide ions were attracted to the electrode surface to participate electrochemical reaction.

There have been reported that the PL property of GQDs is related to the inner sp2 state and surface sp3 state [31,32]. Surface sp3 state is influenced by surface oxygen functional groups. So, to further investigate their luminescent performances, the PL spectra are investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 , both GQDs and rGQDs have similar emission peak at 435 nm, 439 nm, 450 nm and 481 nm, with the excitation wavelength at 320 nm (Fig. 6a), 340 nm (Fig. 6b), 360 nm (Fig. 6c) and 380 nm (Fig. 6d). RGQDs have higher PL intensity than GQDs, indicating surface sp3 state composed of surface oxygen functional groups inhibited intrinsic emission of GQDs. Besides, the quantum yields of GQDs and rGQDs-1 to GQDs-3 were calculated to be 4.40% ± 0.02%, 6.25% ± 0.02%, 5.16% ± 0.02% and 5.05% ± 0.02%, respectively, consistent with the PL intensity. From the results, it is known that after reduction, PLQY were enhanced. As increasing reduced degree, PLQY first increased then decreased. It was implied that whole surface oxygen level influence PL property of GQDs for one hand, surface defect caused by the edge effect after oxygen removing also influenced their PL performance.

To further investigated the reason for different luminescence performance of GQDs and rGQDs, high resolution O1s peaks in the XPS of GQDs, rGQDs-1, rGQDs-2 and rGQDs-3 (Fig. 7a, b, c and d) were conducted. The O1s peak could be mainly deconvoluted into four types of oxygen bonds: CO (531.1–537.7 eV), OCO (532.5 eV), C-OH (533.2 eV) [33–35]. First of all, the content of CO bond decreased gradually from GQDs (74.67%) to rGQDs-1 (25.37%), rGQDs-2 (30.07%), rGQDs-3 (31.61%), suggesting that CO bond is easy to be reduced by NaBH4. Interestingly, the ratio of OCO band is increased 29.4% from GQDs (6.35%) to rGQDs-3 (35.75%). This result implied that O=C-O bond is difficult to be reduced compared with CO bond. Besides, after reduced, the related content of C-OH bond increased obviously, from GQDs (6.31%) to rGQDs-1 (20.61%), rGQDs-2 (33.93%), rGQDs-3 (32.64%). These results indicated that after reduction, although the total content of oxygen in rGQDs was reduced, their relative content of each oxygen functional groups have increased or decreased with different degree.

The variety of luminescence of quantum dots is inseparable from the change of surface state. Based on the above UV–Vis, PLE and PL results, along with the changes in surface oxygen functional groups, it seems that surface hybridization state (surface hybridization structure contained the connected graphene core and covalently bonded carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl as well as epoxy groups [36].) is the principal mode of GQDs and rGQDs. The detailed explanation is below. First of all, from the almost same UV–Vis and PLE spectra of both GQDs and rGQDs, we can know that the band gap of GQDs have almost no change after reduction. Thus, the band gap transition is excluded for the PL change. Then, from the XPS result, rGQDs have high contents of hydroxyl group than original GQDs. From our previous research, hydroxyl group related hybridization structure are blue PL center of GQDs [37], thus more hydroxyl group related hybridization structure will provide strong PL emission of rGQDs. Therefore, after reduction, the PL performance and PLQY of all rGQDs have enhanced, further confirming hydroxyl groups promote fluorescence emission of GQDs. Finally, besides the hydroxyl group related hybridization structure, carboxyl group related hybridization structure also favor for PL emission [36]. From Fig. 6, rGQDs-1 has the highest content of carboxyl groups, and rGQDs-2 lower than rGQDs-1, but rGQDs-3 was the lowest. Thus, accompanied with the change of surface hybridization structure, the PLQY of rGQDs decreased from rGQDs-1 to rGQDs-3. Therefore, the investigation of PL behavior of electrochemical prepared GQDs by controlling their surface oxygen functional groups, further demonstrated that surface hybridization states played mainly role on their PL performance. We hope our work could provide important reference to PL mechanism of GQDs with surface oxygen functional groups.

In this paper, we prepared GQDs by electrochemical method in a green, mild and universal method. The GQDs are reduced in chemical approach to obtain rGQDs with different reduction level. After reduced, the PLQY of rGQDs showed an increasing and then weakening trend by increased reduced degree. By analyzing Raman, FT-IR and XPS spectra, both surface hydroxyl group related hybridization structure and carboxyl group related hybridization structure contribute to the PL emission. This result further confirmed surface hybridization state played main role on PL emission of GQDs with surface oxygen functional groups.

Yan Li and Wenhui Kong designed experiments and wrote the manuscript. Wenhui Kong, Yingmin Wang, Lifeng Wang, Yong Li and Wendong Xue performed the experiments. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Prof. Yapei Wang and Dr. Naiwei Gao at the Renmin University of China for the AFM measurement. This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21674011) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (FRF-GF-17-B11).
